specific_claim_paraphrase: Mean relative fitness of antibiotic resistance mutations across 128 studies = 0.880 (significantly < 1, z=129.3, p<0.0001); Gram-positive mean = 0.822 vs Gram-negative 0.973 (t=-5.19, p<0.0001). Paper concludes resistance mutations 'almost always carry fitness costs' — load-bearing for the field's view that fitness costs constrain resistance spread.
notes: Meta-analysis pulling together studies that themselves used growth-rate fits or competition assays. Underlying studies overwhelmingly use SE not full CI on the input fitness estimates; widening CIs of constituent studies by 30% would likely shrink the apparent significance margin for many comparisons.

specific_claim_paraphrase: OM (omadacycline-resistant E. coli) shows AUC of growth curve ~8x lower than WT; resistant strain reaches stationary density ~5-fold below WT; fitness-recovery evolved line (5X) shows AUC ~5x above OM. Conclusion that 'fitness costs impede further resistance evolution' depends on these AUC contrasts.
parameter_compared: AUC of fitted growth curve, growth rate, stationary OD
reported_uncertainty: SEM (N=16) — no CI on fitted parameters
notes: Load-bearing AUC comparison; SEM only, not CI; many comparisons hover around p<0.01 threshold.

specific_claim_paraphrase: Eight of nine experimentally evolved E. coli lines from a genome-reduced ancestor showed 'highly significant' growth-rate improvement; 'primarily decreased growth rate caused by genome reduction was significantly improved by experimental evolution.'
reported_uncertainty: standard errors from N=4-6 biological reps
notes: Eight-of-nine claim depends on per-line significance test. With 30% wider CIs at least the borderline lines among the nine would likely fall to nonsignificant.

specific_claim_paraphrase: L. monocytogenes growth rate at 12°C: μ_max = 0.1126 (95% CI 0.1049-0.1480) h⁻¹ for strain 954; at 4.5°C: 0.0410 (95% CI 0.0364-0.0459) h⁻¹. Authors stress that omnibus (mixed-effects) modelling produces 'wider CIs than sequential approaches,' arguing the omnibus framework should drive temperature-based shelf-life predictions.
comparison_method: one-way ANOVA + Tukey HSD; mixed-effects omnibus model
notes: Rare paper that *explicitly* reports proper CIs and acknowledges they widen with proper handling — a good example of what good practice looks like; their own argument is *aligned with our paper's thesis*.

specific_claim_paraphrase: Baranyi-fit μ_max and lag time of L. monocytogenes on fresh pork modelled vs storage temperature 5-35°C; secondary models drive shelf-life recommendations. Relative-error proportions of 0.79 (SGR) and 0.18 (LT) used as validation.
parameter_compared: SGR (specific growth rate) and lag time
reported_uncertainty: relative error, standard error of prediction
comparison_method: primary Baranyi fit + secondary models (Ratkowsky / Davey)
notes: Industrial shelf-life recommendation rests on SGR/LT estimates; CIs not reported on μ_max — raw SE only at validation stage.

specific_claim_paraphrase: DDAC dose-response: B. cereus μ falls from ~0.6 to ~0.4 /h between 0 and 0.5 mg/L; computed MIC = 1.39 mg/L (B. cereus) and 2.86 mg/L (E. coli). MIC drives regulatory recommendations on biocide use.
parameter_compared: growth rate as f(DDAC concentration), MIC
comparison_method: max log-likelihood; AIC/BIC for model selection
notes: Some CIs given are notoriously wide (CI > point estimate) — already evidence of overfitted/miscalibrated parameter ID. A 30% widening would push CIs across zero for several rate coefficients.

specific_claim_paraphrase: Proposed method: fit modified Gompertz to OD vs antimicrobial concentration to extract MIC and NIC. Used widely in EUCAST-adjacent susceptibility testing.
reported_uncertainty: mentions 'more exact'; no formal CI in abstract
notes: Foundational MIC-from-growth-curve method; downstream papers using this rarely propagate uncertainty in MIC. A 30% wider MIC CI would change susceptible/resistant categorical calls for borderline isolates.

specific_claim_paraphrase: Growthcurver R package: fits logistic to OD600, returning r (intrinsic growth rate), K (carrying capacity), N0. 937 growth curves, 33 E. coli K12 strains; r and K used directly to *compare* strains. Package does *not* report parameter CIs in its core output.
comparison_method: point comparison; users typically run ad-hoc t-test on replicate-level r/K
notes: Heavily-cited tool. The vast majority of downstream papers using growthcurver compare r and K with simple t-tests on replicate-level point estimates — these ignore within-curve fit uncertainty and would be most directly affected by a CI-recalibration argument.

specific_claim_paraphrase: Broad metabolic-sensitivity profiling of prototrophic yeast deletion collection — growth-rate phenotypes across 16 conditions classified as 'hit' if statistically lower than WT; thousands of mutant-condition pairs scored.
parameter_compared: growth rate (slope of log OD) vs WT
notes: Genome-wide hit-calling: every deletion strain whose growth rate differs significantly from WT becomes a 'gene-function' assignment. False-positive rate is directly tied to CI calibration.

specific_claim_paraphrase: P. aeruginosa starting strains averaged μ_max = 0.37 ± 0.03 /h; after ALE: strain 141_CP = 1.28 ± 0.02 /h; 10_CP = 1.08 ± 0.02 /h; 427.1_CP = 0.87 ± 0.02 /h. Three strains plotted as significantly different fitness improvements — conclusion that slow-growth CF-adapted phenotypes are evolutionarily reversible.
comparison_method: power-law model + bootstrap CI; comparing per-strain trajectories
notes: One of the cleaner examples of bootstrap-based CIs on the parameter. Differences between final μ_max values are large (>3x) so robust; but per-generation comparisons during evolution are within 1.5x and would tighten/loosen with CI change.

specific_claim_paraphrase: 34 S. aureus strains compared at 17°C: μ_max range 0.07-0.24 /h (mean 0.13 ± 0.05 /h); lag range 1.2-25.2 h. Strain-to-strain variation in fitted parameters used to argue for strain heterogeneity in predictive models.
reported_uncertainty: 95% CI on means; SD across strains
comparison_method: ANOVA on method and biotype; two-sample t for method comparison
notes: Strain heterogeneity in fitted μ_max is the load-bearing claim. The 95% CI on the cross-strain mean for the DD method spans −6.7 to 9.6 /h (lag) which is implausibly wide — already a red flag for fit-stability.

specific_claim_paraphrase: REMA-GC method for M. tuberculosis growth kinetics: MDR isolate 06-235 had the slowest growth rate among 21 clinical isolates; lag phase 99% CI = 43-131 h, growth rate 99% CI = 18-27 h. Used to argue resistance ↔ slow growth fitness cost.
notes: Wide CIs already reported. The qualitative 'MDR is slowest' claim might survive 30% CI inflation but quantitative ranking among the 21 isolates would shuffle.

specific_claim_paraphrase: Variability of plasmid fitness effects across hosts: distribution of relative fitness (plasmid+ vs plasmid-) measured across ~50 enterobacterial host strains; argues plasmid persistence is explained by tail of nearly-neutral fitness effects.
reported_uncertainty: SE across triplicates; tests vs neutrality
notes: Conclusion (existence of a 'tail' of beneficial plasmid carriers) rests on a *small subset* of strains scoring fitness >1. With wider CIs more of those strains would be reclassified as nominally neutral, undermining the distributional argument.

specific_claim_paraphrase: Trade-off between lag time and growth rate drives plasmid acquisition cost; acquisition cost and lag show strong positive correlation while growth rate is uncorrelated with cost across plasmids/antibiotics. Argues a particular kinetic decomposition of the cost.
notes: Correlation-based conclusion; uncertainty on individual μ_max points propagates into correlation uncertainty rarely shown.

specific_claim_paraphrase: Colistin-resistant E. coli (chromosome- vs plasmid-mediated) show no significant difference in growth rate vs susceptible parents, but competition index (CI) <1 for chromosomal mutants. Conclusion: 'no fitness cost in monoculture growth rate' is load-bearing for the recommendation that plasmid-mediated colistin resistance can persist.
notes: Negative claim ('no significant difference') is *especially* sensitive to CI calibration — undercalibrated CIs make Type II errors. With 30% wider CIs the null result is reinforced, but power to detect small costs declines.

specific_claim_paraphrase: 6 antibiotics (cefoxitin, daptomycin, fosfomycin, fusidic acid, moxifloxacin, vancomycin) show different MIC at 20h vs 48h based on kinetic growth-curve readouts; fosfomycin at 8 μg/mL gives 'considerably higher' μ_max than control after 31 h.
parameter_compared: μ_max, lag, final OD as f(antibiotic, time)
reported_uncertainty: SD selectively shown in figures only
comparison_method: descriptive — visual comparison of growth curves
notes: No CI on any kinetic parameter — qualitative 'considerably higher' claim drives the central argument. Direct exemplar of the under-reporting problem.

specific_claim_paraphrase: E. coli ALE end-point strains: μ rises from 0.7 /h (WT) to 0.95-1.10 /h; q_glc and q_ac span 3.9-11.4 mmol/gDW/h; claims a 'two-dimensional rate-yield tradeoff' from these comparisons.
parameter_compared: μ_max, biomass yield Y, q_glc, q_ac
notes: Two-dim tradeoff argument depends on observing different Y at similar μ. With only duplicate SDs and no formal CIs, the apparent decoupling of Y and μ could partly reflect under-estimated uncertainty.

specific_claim_paraphrase: Studies routinely score 10-20% growth-rate reduction in rifampicin-resistant strains vs WT using Baranyi/Gompertz fits at α=0.05, supporting strain-level fitness-cost claims.
notes: Representative class of papers. The 10-20% effect size is exactly the regime where 30% wider CIs would flip many claims to non-significant.
